If you or someone you know has been injured in a rideshare accident in San Antonio, Texas, the path to fair compensation is rarely straightforward.

Uber and Lyft accidents involve layered insurance policies that shift depending on whether the driver was logged into the app, waiting for a ride request, or actively transporting a passenger. In Texas, that distinction can mean the difference between a $50,000 policy and a $1,000,000 one — and insurance adjusters know exactly how to exploit the confusion.

At WreckMatch, we connect injured victims with attorneys from our network of 800+ law firms across the country who understand rideshare litigation, Texas liability rules, and how to hold the right parties accountable.

Here is what experienced rideshare accident attorneys can help with:
• Identifying all liable parties including Uber, Lyft, the driver, and third parties
• Navigating multi-layered insurance coverage
• Preserving critical digital evidence before it disappears
• Maximizing compensation for medical bills, lost wages, and pain and suffering

If you were hurt in a San Antonio rideshare accident, do not wait. Evidence fades and deadlines apply.

reddit_title: Got hit while riding in an Uber/Lyft in San Antonio — whose insurance actually covers me in Texas?
reddit_body:
If you were injured as a passenger in a rideshare accident in San Antonio, the coverage that applies depends heavily on the driver's status at the time of the crash. Texas law recognizes three distinct phases: driver offline (only personal insurance applies), driver online but waiting for a request (limited contingent liability coverage through Uber or Lyft), and driver actively on a trip (full $1,000,000 commercial policy kicks in). Most victims don't realize the phase matters so much until an adjuster uses it against them.

A few things to do immediately after a rideshare crash: call 911 and get a police report, screenshot your trip details in the app before closing it, collect witness contact info, and seek medical attention even if you feel fine. Do not give a recorded statement to any insurance company — yours, Uber's, or Lyft's — without talking to an attorney first. Insurers move fast and early statements can be used to minimize your payout.

If you are trying to figure out next steps, it helps to talk to an attorney who specifically handles rideshare cases in Texas. The site wreckmatch.com lets you get matched with experienced local attorneys for free — no obligation. Worth checking out if you want someone in your corner who knows how this works.
